title: Breaking \(e^n\) barrier for deterministic poly-time approximation of the permanent and settling Friedland's conjecture on the Monomer-Dimer Entropy
speaker: Leonid Gurvits
abstract: Two important breakthroughs on the permanent had been accomplished in 1998: A. Schrijver proved Schrijver-Valiant Conjecture on the minimal exponential growth of the number of perfect matchings in k-regular bipartite graphs with multiple edges; N. Linial, A. Samorodnitsky and A. Wigderson introduced a strongly poly-time deterministic algorithm to approximate the permanent of general non-negative matrices within the multiplicative factor en. Many things happened since them, notably the prize-winning Jerrum, Vigoda, Sinclair FPRAS for the permanent.

proof_stages.label: Permanent and coefficient frameworks
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines permanents, subpermanents and matching polynomials, then places them in product-polynomial, Fock-space, and determinant-norm frameworks that also include mixed discriminants and positive-semidefinite permanents.

proof_stages.label: Stable-polynomial capacity method and its limitation
proof_stages.summary: Permanents and related objects are recast as mixed derivatives of homogeneous nonnegative polynomials, capacities and derivative chains are introduced, and the stable-polynomial method is shown to give computable simple-exponential bounds but not to break the e^n barrier because it discards matrix structure.

proof_stages.label: Earlier matrix scaling and Schrijver lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the Linial-Samorodnitsky-Wigderson scaling approximation, Barvinok-type randomized determinant estimators, and Schrijver’s difficult lower bounds for regular bipartite graph permanents as the matrix-level ingredients behind the new approach.

proof_stages.label: Monomer-dimer entropy target
proof_stages.summary: The monomer-dimer problem is formulated as minimizing sums of m-subpermanents, equivalently numbers of matchings of a given size in k-regular bipartite graphs, and Friedland’s conjectural entropy limit is identified together with a related data-dependent lower-bound conjecture.

proof_stages.label: New Schrijver-type entrywise permanent inequality
proof_stages.summary: The central inequality gives a lower bound for the permanent of a doubly stochastic matrix by the entrywise product of (1-a_{ij})^{1-a_{ij}}, is compared with earlier conjectured bounds, and is explained as equivalent to or derived from Schrijver’s inequality.

proof_stages.label: Bethe functional and deterministic scaling approximation
proof_stages.summary: A modified KL/Bethe variational functional from belief propagation is introduced, the permanent is bounded below by its maximum over doubly stochastic matrices, and ordinary Sinkhorn scaling plus this bound yields a deterministic polynomial-time approximation with a simple-exponential factor below the previous e^n barrier.

proof_stages.label: Friedland conjecture via graph expansion
proof_stages.summary: Sums of subpermanents are expressed as a single permanent of an expanded matrix, that matrix is explicitly scaled to the doubly stochastic setting, and the new lower bound is applied to settle Friedland’s monomer-dimer entropy conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Further consequences and probabilistic outlook
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by describing counterexamples to the related Lu-Moore-Székely positive-correlation conjecture, giving a probabilistic interpretation of permanents as large-deviation probabilities, and suggesting future combinations of the new bounds with stable-polynomial methods.

examples.name: Linear-quantum-optics permanent distribution
examples.note: A fixed unitary matrix gives a permanent polynomial with nonnegative coefficients summing to one, illustrating why permanents encode probability distributions in linear optical sampling.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the permanent polynomial from a unitary-generated matrix class and checks properties such as nonnegative coefficients, value at the all-ones vector, and doubly-stochastic gradient.

passes.reason: A unitary-generated permanent polynomial is used as a standard boson-sampling probability distribution and motivation, not as a structurally sporadic or adversarial object.

A unitary-derived permanent probability distribution is used as quantum-optics motivation, with no maximal, collapsed, extremal, or sporadic structure indicated.

Boson-sampling/permanent output distribution from a unitary is used as a motivating application, with no intrinsic exceptional or extremal structure specified.

Used as an application of permanents in linear quantum optics; a generic unitary-induced probability distribution is not intrinsically extremal, degenerate, maximally symmetric, or sporadic.

examples.name: Traveling-salesman polynomial for degree hardness
examples.note: A polynomial encoding Hamiltonian tours is used to illustrate why computing variable degrees of general nonnegative polynomials is hard without the stability structure.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes down the TSP-related polynomial construction and explains how its mixed derivative and partial derivatives encode Hamiltonian-tour existence to show degree computation is hard.

passes.reason: The graph-trace polynomial encoding Hamiltonian tours is deployed as a hardness obstruction to efficient degree computation.

The traveling-salesman polynomial is used as a hardness gadget showing general degree computation can fail to be easy, while being structurally non-sporadic and not extremal.
The TSP/Hamiltonian-tour polynomial is used as a hardness gadget showing degree computation can fail to be easy.
The TSP/Hamiltonian-tour polynomial is used as a hard adversarial instance showing degree computation is hard in general.
The traveling-salesman/Hamiltonian-cycle polynomial is an ordinary encoding gadget used to show that computing degrees can be hard.
Used as a hard/adversarial polynomial encoding Hamiltonian tours to show degree computation cannot generally be easy; the encoding itself is a systematic, typical hardness construction.

The TSP/Hamiltonian-cycle polynomial is used as a hardness obstruction to computing degrees, but is otherwise a generic reduction gadget.
The TSP/Hamiltonian-tour polynomial is an adversarial encoding used to show degree computation is hard.
the Hamiltonian-tour/TSP polynomial is used as a hardness gadget showing degree computation cannot be easy in general
used as a Hamiltonian-tour encoding that obstructs general polynomial-time degree computation

examples.name: Bounded-sparsity permanent bounds
examples.note: Matrices with, say, only a few nonzero entries per column illustrate that the new Schrijver-type bound depends on support sparsity rather than on integrality or regularity alone.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the new permanent lower-bound functional to sparse matrices, explaining the plug-in/scaling step and how the resulting bound depends only on sparsity.

passes.reason: Bounded-sparsity matrices form an ordinary sparse family used to illustrate improved permanent lower bounds depending on support size.
The bounded-sparsity setting concerns low-support matrices compared with generic dense matrices, and is presented as an application of the new bounds.
The bounded-sparsity permanent setting has atypically small support compared with a general matrix and is used for positive lower bounds rather than to refute a claim.
Bounded-sparsity matrices are a restricted but systematic family used to illustrate improved permanent bounds, not to refute a claim.
Bounded-sparsity matrices are discussed as an application of improved permanent bounds rather than as sporadic, extremal, or refuting examples.
The bounded-sparsity matrices have atypically small support, but the sparsity-dependent permanent bounds are presented as positive estimates rather than refutations.
Used to illustrate improved permanent lower bounds depending on sparsity, not to refute a claim; bounded-sparsity matrices lie on a sparse/boundary subfamily with many forced zeros.
the sparse-matrix regime has reduced support and the discussion concerns extremal lower bounds for permanents, not a refutation
Bounded-sparsity matrices are used as an application where the permanent bounds improve, not to refute a claim, and no listed special structure is intrinsic.
Bounded-sparsity matrices have reduced support and are used as an application of the new permanent bounds.
sparsity-dependent permanent estimates concern sparse boundary instances and lower/tight minima, not a failure of a claim
used as a positive sparse-matrix regime for improved permanent bounds, with bounded support a low-density limiting case

examples.name: Same half-entry multiset with different permanents
examples.note: Arranging the same collection of entries equal to one half in different doubly stochastic matrices gives permanents differing by about 2^{n/2}, motivating the conjectural root-two-per-dimension optimal barrier for entry-list-based approximation.
examples.engagement_reason: He compares two doubly stochastic matrices with the same multiset of half-entries and computes their different permanents to justify a lower limit on approximation from entry data alone.

passes.reason: The equal-half cycle/block arrangements are boundary cases with the same entry multiset but extremally different permanents, ruling out better multiset-only approximation.
The two doubly stochastic half-entry matrices have very sparse boundary support and are arranged to give an extremal separation, blocking any better entry-multiset-only estimate.
The half-entry/zero doubly stochastic arrangements are sparse boundary cases giving the adversarial near-$(\sqrt2)^n$ separation from the same entry multiset.
The very sparse 0/1/2 doubly stochastic matrices give a tight information-theoretic obstruction to approximating from the entry multiset alone.
The two half-entry doubly stochastic matrices give an extremal indistinguishability barrier for multiset-only approximation and hence obstruct better-than-root-two-to-the-n guarantees.
The same-multiset 0/1/2 doubly stochastic arrangements lie on a sparse boundary and give the critical sqrt(2)^n obstruction to using only the entry multiset.

Used as an obstruction to any multiset-only approximation better than about sqrt(2)^n; the half-entry sparse doubly stochastic arrangements are boundary cases and realize extreme permanent values within that fixed multiset class.
sparse 0/1/2 doubly stochastic arrangements with the same entries give a worst-case permanent gap obstructing multiset-only approximation

The two 0–1/2 doubly stochastic arrangements with the same multiset give an adversarial lower bound for entry-multiset approximation and realize extreme permanent values among such 2-regular supports.
The sparse 0-and-1/2 doubly stochastic arrangements achieve the sqrt-2-to-the-n separation blocking better multiset-only approximation.

examples.name: Structured counterexamples to the Lu-Moore-Székely conjecture
examples.note: Special doubly stochastic matrices arising from sums of subpermanents, unlike typical random doubly stochastic matrices near the uniform matrix, reveal that the proposed positive-correlation lower bound is false.
examples.engagement_reason: He says structured large doubly stochastic counterexamples to the Lu-Moore-Székely conjecture exist and sketches their provenance, but does not display or verify one.

passes.reason: The explicitly constructed doubly stochastic matrices are introduced precisely because they violate the Lu-Moore-Székely conjectured lower bound.
These specially constructed doubly stochastic matrices are explicitly used to disprove the Lu-Moore-Székely permanent conjecture.

examples.name: Row-stochastic permanent as a large-deviation probability
examples.note: Viewing the rows of a stochastic matrix as independent basis-valued random vectors makes the permanent the probability that their sum hits its expectation, illustrating a probabilistic large-deviation perspective on permanent inequalities.
examples.engagement_reason: He builds the row-stochastic interpretation by assigning row distributions to independent basis-valued random vectors and identifies the permanent with the probability of the sum hitting its expectation.

passes.reason: A row-stochastic matrix is reinterpreted as independent categorical trials so the permanent is a probability of hitting the expectation, not a special or refuting case.
The row-stochastic interpretation is the ordinary distribution of sums of independent categorical basis vectors, used to rephrase the permanent probabilistically.
A row-stochastic matrix is reinterpreted probabilistically as a sum of independent basis-valued variables, not used as a failure case or special extreme object.
A row-stochastic matrix is reinterpreted probabilistically so its permanent is a large-deviation probability, not used adversarially.
A row-stochastic permanent is recast as the probability that a sum of independent basis-valued variables hits its expectation, serving as an interpretation rather than a refutation.
A row-stochastic permanent viewed as the probability that independent categorical sums hit their mean is a standard probabilistic interpretation, not a special or adversarial case.
Used as a probabilistic reinterpretation of the permanent as a large-deviation probability for independent categorical variables; a row-stochastic matrix in this role is generic.
the row-stochastic interpretation recasts the permanent as a probability of a sum hitting its expectation, serving as intuition rather than a counterexample
